nodes.title: Define a relation as a subset of A x B
nodes.brief: A relation from A to B is any subset of A × B — the mathematical abstraction drops the requirement for a real-world 'link' between the elements.
nodes.quote: we define mathematically a relation R from A to B as an arbitrary subset of
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The universe: the Cartesian product A × B.
The Cartesian product A × B is the set of ALL ordered pairs (a, b) with a in A and b in B. If A = {1, 2} and B = {x, y}, then A × B = {(1,x), (1,y), (2,x), (2,y)}: four pairs. This is the universe from which any relation is carved out.

PART 2 — A relation is any subset of A × B.
A relation R from A to B is defined as any chosen subset of A × B — no conditions required. The empty set is a valid relation (relating nothing). The whole of A × B is a valid relation (relating everything). Any partial choice of pairs is a relation. Mathematics places no constraint on which pairs you pick.

PART 3 — Reading membership as 'a is related to b'.
If the pair (a, b) belongs to R we say 'a is related to b' and write a R b. If (a, b) is not in R, a is not related to b. No real-world link between a and b is required — membership in R is the entire definition. A relation in a set A is the special case B = A: a subset of A × A.
keyMoves: Identify A × B as the set of ALL ordered pairs (a, b) with a in A and b in B — the universe of the relation
Pick any subset of A × B as R; no rule or real-world link is required
Read (a, b) ∈ R as 'a is related to b' (write a R b); (a, b) ∉ R means a is not related to b
misconceptions: Thinking a relation needs a meaningful real-world connection between the elements
Confusing the relation R (a set of pairs) with the rule used to describe it

nodes.title: Recognise the empty relation
nodes.brief: Identify the empty relation: the relation containing no pairs, where no element of A is related to any element of A.
nodes.quote: A relation R in a set A is called *empty relation*, if no element of A is related to any element of A
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What makes a relation empty.
The empty relation in a set A is R = ∅ — no ordered pair (a, b) from A × A belongs to R, so no element of A is related to any other element of A. It is still a valid relation; it is simply the smallest possible one.

PART 2 — Testing: check whether ANY pair satisfies the condition.
To decide if a relation is empty, ask: is there even a single pair (a, b) in A × A that satisfies the defining rule? If the answer is no for every possible pair, the relation is empty. Example: on A = {1, 2, 3, 4}, the rule 'a − b = 10' is satisfied by no pair (maximum of a − b is 4 − 1 = 3), so R = ∅.

PART 3 — Position: one of the two trivial extremes.
The empty relation and the universal relation (R = A × A) are the two extreme cases, sometimes called trivial relations, because all other relations lie strictly between them. Both are always reflexive-or-not, symmetric-or-not in a trivial way; exam questions often exclude them when asking for 'interesting' examples.
keyMoves: Check whether ANY pair (a, b) in A × A satisfies the defining condition
If no pair satisfies it, declare the relation empty: R = ∅
Recognise the empty relation as one of the two trivial extremes (the other is the universal relation R = A × A)
misconceptions: Confusing 'empty relation' (no pairs) with a relation that simply has few pairs
Thinking a relation that holds for no pairs is not a valid relation

nodes.title: Recognise the universal relation
nodes.brief: Identify the universal relation: the relation A × A in which every element of A is related to every element of A.
nodes.quote: A relation R in a set A is called *universal relation*, if each element of A is related to every element of A
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What makes a relation universal.
The universal relation in a set A is R = A × A — every possible ordered pair (a, b) with a, b in A is included. It is the largest relation on A; every other relation on A is a subset of it.

PART 2 — Testing: check whether EVERY pair satisfies the condition.
A relation is universal if its defining rule is satisfied by ALL pairs (a, b) in A × A — not just many, but every pair without exception. Example: on A = {1, 2, 3, 4}, the rule '|a − b| ≥ 0' is satisfied by every pair (since |a − b| is always non-negative), so R = A × A.

PART 3 — Position: the second trivial extreme.
The universal relation is the counterpart to the empty relation: empty gives minimum (no pairs), universal gives maximum (all pairs). Any 'interesting' relation on A is a proper subset strictly between the two. Note: the universal relation always includes self-pairs (a, a) — it is always reflexive and symmetric.
keyMoves: Check whether EVERY pair (a, b) in A × A satisfies the defining condition
If all do, declare the relation universal: R = A × A
Recognise the universal relation as the second trivial extreme; it is always reflexive and symmetric
misconceptions: Thinking 'universal' means a relation across different sets rather than R = A x A
Forgetting that the universal relation includes pairs (a, a) as well

nodes.title: Test a relation for reflexivity
nodes.brief: Decide whether a relation is reflexive: every element must be related to itself, (a, a) ∈ R for every a in A.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What reflexivity requires.
A relation R in a set A is reflexive if (a, a) ∈ R for EVERY element a in A — every element must be related to itself, without exception. For A = {1, 2, 3}, the required self-pairs are exactly (1,1), (2,2), (3,3); all three must appear in R.

PART 2 — Testing: list and check every required self-pair.
Step 1: list all self-pairs (a, a) for every a in A. Step 2: check that each one is present in R. If even one is missing, reflexivity fails. Example: R = {(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,3)} on {1,2,3} is reflexive — all self-pairs present. R = {(1,1),(2,3)} is NOT reflexive: (2,2) and (3,3) are missing.

PART 3 — One missing self-pair is enough to fail.
Extra pairs in R beyond the self-pairs are irrelevant to reflexivity. The only check is whether every diagonal element is covered. Real-world counterexample: 'is perpendicular to' among lines is not reflexive because no line is perpendicular to itself — every self-pair is absent.
keyMoves: List the required self-pairs (a, a) for every a in A
Check that every (a, a) is present in R
Conclude reflexive only if ALL self-pairs are present; one missing self-pair fails the test
misconceptions: Thinking one self-pair (a, a) in R is enough; reflexivity needs ALL of them
Confusing reflexive (a related to itself) with symmetric

nodes.title: Test a relation for symmetry
nodes.brief: Decide whether a relation is symmetric: whenever (a₁, a₂) is in R, the reverse (a₂, a₁) must also be in R.
nodes.quote: symmetric, if  $(a_1, a_2) \in \mathbb{R}$  implies that  $(a_2, a_1) \in \mathbb{R}$ , for all
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What symmetry requires.
A relation R in a set A is symmetric if: whenever (a₁, a₂) ∈ R, the reverse (a₂, a₁) also ∈ R, for ALL pairs in R. Every pair must come with its mirror image. Self-pairs (a, a) are automatically their own reverse and impose no extra obligation.

PART 2 — Testing: for each pair, look for its reverse.
For every pair (a, b) in R, check whether (b, a) is also in R. If any pair is 'one-sided' — (a, b) present but (b, a) absent — symmetry fails immediately. Example: R = {(1,2),(2,1),(3,3)} on {1,2,3} is symmetric (each non-self pair has its reverse). R = {(1,2),(2,3)} is NOT symmetric: (2,1) and (3,2) are both absent.

PART 3 — Real-world test and contrast with reflexivity.
'Is perpendicular to' is symmetric: L₁ ⊥ L₂ implies L₂ ⊥ L₁. 'Is brother of' is not: A is brother of B does not imply B is brother of A (B might be a sister). Symmetry and reflexivity are independent properties — a relation can have either, both, or neither.
keyMoves: For each pair (a, b) in R, check whether the reverse (b, a) is also in R
If any (a, b) is present but (b, a) is absent, the relation is NOT symmetric
Conclude symmetric only if every non-self pair has its reverse present in R
misconceptions: Checking only some pairs instead of all of them
Confusing symmetry (reverse the pair) with reflexivity (self-pair)

nodes.title: Test a relation for transitivity
nodes.brief: Decide whether a relation is transitive: if (a1, a2) and (a2, a3) are in R, then (a1, a3) must be in R.
nodes.quote: transitive, if  $(a_1, a_2) \in \mathbb{R}$  and  $(a_2, a_3) \in \mathbb{R}$  implies that  $(a_1, a_3) \in \mathbb{R}$
nodes.explanation: A relation R in a set A is transitive if (a1, a2) in R and (a2, a3) in R together imply (a1, a3) in R, for all a1, a2, a3 in A - relations chain through. To test it, find every pair of links (a, b) and (b, c) in R and check whether the shortcut (a, c) is present. 'Congruent to' is transitive; 'perpendicular to' is not (L1 perp L2 and L2 perp L3 give L1 parallel to L3, not perpendicular).
keyMoves: Find every chain (a, b) and (b, c) inside R
Check the implied pair (a, c) is in R for each chain
Conclude transitive only if no implied pair is missing
misconceptions: Forgetting to check chains that share a middle element
Assuming a relation that is reflexive and symmetric is automatically transitive

nodes.title: Define and prove an equivalence relation
nodes.brief: Recognise an equivalence relation as one that is reflexive, symmetric AND transitive, and prove it by checking all three.
nodes.quote: A relation R in a set A is said to be an *equivalence relation* if R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
nodes.explanation: An equivalence relation is the most important type of relation: a relation R in a set A that is reflexive, symmetric and transitive all at once. To prove a relation is an equivalence relation you verify the three properties in turn. For example R = {(a, b) : 2 divides a - b} on the integers is reflexive (2 divides a - a), symmetric (2 divides a - b implies 2 divides b - a), and transitive (a - c = (a - b) + (b - c) is even), hence an equivalence relation. 'Congruent to' on triangles is another example.
keyMoves: Prove reflexivity: (a, a) in R for all a
Prove symmetry: (a, b) in R implies (b, a) in R
Prove transitivity: (a, b),(b, c) in R imply (a, c) in R; only then declare it an equivalence relation
misconceptions: Concluding 'equivalence relation' after checking only one or two of the three properties
Thinking 'a <= b' or 'perpendicular' are equivalence relations (they fail one property)

nodes.title: Form equivalence classes and partitions
nodes.brief: Understand how an equivalence relation splits a set into disjoint equivalence classes whose union is the whole set.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form the equivalence class [a].
The class [a] is the set of every element in X related to a under R. To find [a], test each b in X: if (a, b) ∈ R then b ∈ [a]. For R = {(a, b) : 2 | (a − b)} on Z, the class [0] contains every even integer and [1] contains every odd integer.

PART 2 — Verify the classes partition X.
Two facts follow from the properties of an equivalence relation: (i) every element x is in at least one class (x ∈ [x] by reflexivity); (ii) any two classes are either identical or completely disjoint — they never partially overlap. Together the classes tile X without gaps or repetitions: a partition.

PART 3 — Assign an element to its class.
Given element c, test whether (c, representative) ∈ R for each candidate class representative. If yes, c belongs to that class. Example: for mod-2 above, does 7 belong to [0] or [1]? 7 − 0 = 7 (odd, not divisible by 2) so 7 ∉ [0]. 7 − 1 = 6 (divisible by 2) so 7 ∈ [1].
keyMoves: Group together all elements related to a given element to form its class [a]
Check the classes are disjoint and cover all of X (a partition)
Use the relation to decide which class an element lands in
misconceptions: Thinking equivalence classes can overlap
Believing [0] and [1] are equal in the mod-2 example; they are distinct classes

nodes.title: Test a function for one-one (injective)
nodes.brief: Decide whether a function is one-one: distinct inputs give distinct outputs, i.e. f(x1) = f(x2) implies x1 = x2.
nodes.quote: A function  $f: X \to Y$  is defined to be *one-one* (or *injective*), if the images of distinct elements of X under f are distinct
nodes.explanation: A function f : X -> Y is one-one (injective) if distinct elements of X have distinct images - equivalently, f(x1) = f(x2) implies x1 = x2; otherwise it is many-one. To prove one-one, assume f(x1) = f(x2) and deduce x1 = x2. For example f(x) = 2x on the reals is one-one (2x1 = 2x2 gives x1 = x2), while f(x) = x^2 on the reals is not (f(-1) = f(1) = 1).
keyMoves: Assume f(x1) = f(x2) and try to deduce x1 = x2
If a deduction succeeds, the function is one-one
To disprove, exhibit two distinct inputs with the same image (e.g. f(-1) = f(1))
misconceptions: Confusing one-one (injective, about inputs) with onto (surjective, about outputs)
Declaring a function one-one without checking ALL pairs of inputs

nodes.title: Test a function for onto (surjective)
nodes.brief: Decide whether a function is onto: every element of the co-domain Y is the image of some element of X.
nodes.quote: A function  $f: X \to Y$  is said to be *onto* (or *surjective*), if every element of Y is the image of some element of X under f
nodes.explanation: A function f : X -> Y is onto (surjective) if every element y of the co-domain Y is the image of at least one element x of X - equivalently, the range of f equals Y. To prove onto, take an arbitrary y in Y and produce an x in X with f(x) = y. For example f(x) = 2x on the reals is onto (take x = y/2), but f : N -> N with f(x) = 2x is not onto, since 1 is never an image.
keyMoves: Take an arbitrary y in the co-domain Y
Find (or fail to find) an x in X with f(x) = y
Conclude onto iff Range of f = Y
misconceptions: Mixing up the co-domain Y with the range when testing onto
Concluding onto from a few values without covering ALL of Y

nodes.title: Recognise a bijective (one-one and onto) function
nodes.brief: Identify a bijection as a function that is both one-one and onto.
nodes.quote: A function  $f: X \to Y$  is said to be *one-one* and *onto* (or *bijective*), if f is both one-one and onto.
nodes.explanation: A function f : X -> Y is bijective (one-one and onto) when it is simultaneously injective and surjective: distinct inputs give distinct outputs AND every element of Y is hit. To show a function is bijective, prove both properties separately. For finite sets X, a one-one function f : X -> X is automatically onto and vice versa, but this fails for infinite sets (e.g. f(x) = 2x on N is one-one but not onto).
keyMoves: Prove the function is one-one (injective)
Prove the function is onto (surjective)
Conclude bijective only when both hold
misconceptions: Assuming one-one alone makes a function bijective
Carrying the finite-set 'one-one iff onto' shortcut over to infinite sets

nodes.title: Compute the composition of two functions
nodes.brief: Form the composite gof of f : A -> B and g : B -> C by (gof)(x) = g(f(x)).
nodes.quote: the composition of f and g, denoted by *gof*, is defined as the function *gof*: A  $\to C$  given by
nodes.explanation: Given f : A -> B and g : B -> C, the composition gof : A -> C is defined by (gof)(x) = g(f(x)) for all x in A: first apply f, then apply g to the result. To compute it, push each input through f and feed the output into g. Composition is generally NOT commutative: for f(x) = cos x and g(x) = 3x^2, gof(x) = 3 cos^2 x while fog(x) = cos(3x^2), so gof is not equal to fog.
keyMoves: Apply f to the input first to get f(x)
Apply g to that result: (gof)(x) = g(f(x))
Do not assume gof = fog; check the order
misconceptions: Applying g before f when computing gof
Assuming composition is commutative (gof = fog in general)

nodes.title: Define an invertible function and its inverse
nodes.brief: Recognise an invertible function as one with a g satisfying gof = identity on X and fog = identity on Y; g is the inverse f-inverse.
nodes.quote: A function  $f: X \to Y$  is defined to be *invertible*, if there exists a function  $g: Y \to X$  such that
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Define invertibility using two composite identities.
A function f: X→Y is invertible if there exists g: Y→X with g∘f = I_X and f∘g = I_Y. Both identities are required: checking only one is not enough.

PART 2 — Find g by solving y = f(x) for x.
Treat y = f(x) as an equation and solve for x in terms of y. The resulting expression defines g(y). For f(x) = 4x + 3: set y = 4x + 3, solve to get x = (y − 3)/4, so g(y) = (y − 3)/4.

PART 3 — Verify both composite identities.
Check (g∘f)(x) = g(f(x)) = x and (f∘g)(y) = f(g(y)) = y. For the example: (g∘f)(x) = g(4x+3) = (4x+3−3)/4 = x ✓; (f∘g)(y) = f((y−3)/4) = 4·(y−3)/4 + 3 = y ✓. If either check fails, g is not the inverse of f.
keyMoves: Look for g : Y -> X with gof = I_X and fog = I_Y
Find g by solving y = f(x) for x
Verify both composite identities to confirm g = f-inverse
misconceptions: Checking only gof = I_X and forgetting fog = I_Y (or vice versa)
Believing every function has an inverse

nodes.title: Prove a function invertible by showing it is one-one and onto
nodes.brief: Use the key result: f is invertible if and only if f is one-one and onto, so bijectivity is the test for invertibility.
nodes.quote: if f is invertible, then f must be one-one and onto and conversely, if f is one-one and onto, then f must be invertible.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Show f is one-one (injective).
Assume f(x₁) = f(x₂) and deduce x₁ = x₂. If you can always do this, f is injective. For f(x) = 3x − 5: if 3x₁ − 5 = 3x₂ − 5 then x₁ = x₂ ✓.

PART 2 — Show f is onto (surjective).
For any y in the codomain, find at least one x in the domain with f(x) = y. For f(x) = 3x − 5 on ℝ: given any y, set x = (y+5)/3 ∈ ℝ; then f(x) = y ✓.

PART 3 — Invoke 'invertible iff bijective' to conclude.
The theorem states: f is invertible ⟺ f is bijective (one-one AND onto). Having shown both properties, write the conclusion directly — you do not need to construct the explicit inverse. Conversely, if f is NOT onto (or not one-one), you can immediately rule out invertibility.
keyMoves: Show f is one-one (injective)
Show f is onto (surjective)
Invoke 'invertible iff bijective' to conclude f is invertible without building the inverse
misconceptions: Thinking you must construct the explicit inverse to prove invertibility
Claiming invertibility from one-one alone, without onto
